spec_match.rationale: Walks the Sec-Session-Registration header, TPM keypair, signed registration JWT, short-lived cookie, and refresh nonce loop — the exact sequence from the webappsec-dbsc explainer.

interactive.rationale: Play/reset controls advance through the handshake against a mocked server in-tab, with visible step-by-step state changes for each request/response.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature blurb sells 'step through the actual HTTP/JWS exchange from the IETF draft' and the page renders the registration POST, the signed JWT, and the refresh nonce dance.

edge_cases.rationale: Happy-path handshake is covered. Missing: nonce-mismatch on refresh (server rejects), registration retry after TPM transient failure, and the multi-session-per-origin case (Sec-Session-Registration can carry multiple session ids).

references.rationale: Links chromestatus.com/feature/5140168270413824 in the see-also list.

design_system.rationale: Step indicators, code blocks, and buttons all read from CSS variables; no static-card pattern; mock HTTP responses styled with --bg-paper / --border-black.

openQuestions.title: Show the JWT decoded vs raw
openQuestions.detail: The registration JWT and refresh JWT are the core artefacts of the protocol. Currently they appear as base64 strings (or are abbreviated). Add a toggle 'decoded vs base64' that shows the JOSE header (alg, typ, jwk), the claims (aud, jti, exp, iat, nonce), and the signature side-by-side with the raw token. That's what makes the spec text concrete for an implementer.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate nonce-replay rejection
openQuestions.detail: DBSC's refresh nonce is single-use. A 'replay the same nonce twice' button would let authors see the server-side rejection (401 + a fresh nonce challenge) which is the protocol's main anti-replay surface. Without that, the demo gives no visceral feel for why the nonce exists.

openQuestions.title: Add Sec-Session-Id / Sec-Session-Response headers to the wire view
openQuestions.detail: The IETF draft uses Sec-Session-Id on subsequent requests and Sec-Session-Response on the refresh return. The page focuses on Sec-Session-Registration but the steady-state traffic is the other two — show them in the request panels for the post-registration phase.

summary: Solid protocol explainer — the registration + refresh loop is laid out clearly. To make it implementer-grade, decode the JWTs visibly, demo nonce-replay rejection, and surface the Sec-Session-Id / Sec-Session-Response steady-state headers.
